{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        self.pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1)
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(in_ch, FC_HIDDEN),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Dropout(DROPOUT_RATE),
            nn.Linear(FC_HIDDEN, NUM_CLASSES),
        )","old":"        feat_size = IMAGE_SIZE // (2**DEPTH)
        feat_dim = in_ch * feat_size * feat_size
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(feat_dim, FC_HIDDEN),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Dropout(DROPOUT_RATE),
            nn.Linear(FC_HIDDEN, NUM_CLASSES),
        )","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        return self.classifier(self.pool(self.features(x)))","old":"        return self.classifier(self.features(x))","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"After strengthening the convolutional trunk, the next likely bottleneck is the large flattened classifier head. Switching to global average pooling should make the model more parameter-efficient and bias it toward stronger spatial features, which often improves CIFAR-10 validation loss under short training budgets.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        feat_size = IMAGE_SIZE // (2**DEPTH)
        feat_dim = in_ch * feat_size * feat_size
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(feat_dim, FC_HIDDEN),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Dropout(DROPOUT_RATE),
            nn.Linear(FC_HIDDEN, NUM_CLASSES),
        )","old":"        self.pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1)
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(in_ch, FC_HIDDEN),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Dropout(DROPOUT_RATE),
            nn.Linear(FC_HIDDEN, NUM_CLASSES),
        )","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        return self.classifier(self.features(x))","old":"        return self.classifier(self.pool(self.features(x)))","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Global average pooling was clearly harmful here, so the first priority is to restore the stronger flattened head. On top of that rollback, a small amount of label smoothing is a low-complexity regularization change that often improves CIFAR-10 validation loss without disrupting the successful optimizer and convolutional-block improvements.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The largest gains so far have come from increasing feature-extractor capacity while keeping the successful optimizer and schedule intact. A further modest width increase is the simplest next experiment and should test whether this workload is still under-capacity at 16 base channels.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The extra width at 20 channels was slower and worse, so this candidate returns to the stronger 16-channel architecture and tests AdamW as a cleaner optimizer for the same learning-rate schedule. This is a focused optimizer-family change on top of the best-performing architecture seen so far.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"AdamW was slightly worse than the earlier Adam result on this architecture, so this candidate returns to Adam and then makes a small downward learning-rate adjustment. With the wider 16-channel double-convolution trunk already improving capacity, a slightly lower effective LR should test whether the current setup is just overshooting late in the 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Lowering the effective Adam rate from 1e-3 to 8e-4 produced a modest but real gain, which suggests the current setup benefits from slightly gentler updates. The next clean follow-up is another small LR reduction to test whether the optimum sits a bit lower under the same architecture and scheduler.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The lower learning rate moved away from the current optimum, so this candidate restores the stronger 4e-4 setting and then increases only the MLP head width. That is a cheap capacity increase compared with widening the convolutional trunk again, and it may let the improved 16-channel features separate classes better within the same step budget.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Increasing the MLP head size did not help, so this candidate restores the stronger 48-unit head and then relaxes weight decay slightly. With the current network and lower learning rate already close to a good setting, a small reduction in regularization is a simple way to test whether the model is still a bit underfit within 256 steps.","secondary_modes":["layout","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Reducing weight decay from 1e-4 to 5e-5 produced another measurable gain, which suggests the current model is still slightly under-regularized for this short training budget. The clean next probe is to lower weight decay once more and see whether validation loss continues improving along the same direction.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-5","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The weight-decay sweep appears to peak near 5e-5, so this candidate restores that setting and then targets Adam's adaptation dynamics. With only 256 optimization steps, lowering beta2 is a plausible way to make second-moment estimates respond faster and improve short-horizon convergence.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.98)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"Lowering Adam's beta2 from 0.999 to 0.99 improved the short-horizon run, which suggests faster second-moment adaptation is helping. The next logical optimization-family probe is a slightly lower beta2 again to see whether the 256-step regime benefits from even more responsive variance estimates.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.85, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.98)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The beta2 sweep appears to favor 0.99 over 0.98, but the short fixed-step budget may still benefit from slightly less momentum in the first moment estimate. Moving to betas of (0.85, 0.99) keeps the successful faster second-moment adaptation while testing a more responsive update direction.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.85, 0.99)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 80","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The beta1 reduction was not helpful, so this candidate restores the stronger optimizer setting and then uses a modestly larger batch. Under a fixed 256-step budget, increasing batch size is a simple way to expose the model to more examples without changing the core architecture, while 80 is conservative enough to avoid the large slowdown seen with more aggressive capacity changes.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 96","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 80","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The jump from batch size 64 to 80 produced the largest recent gain, which strongly suggests that seeing more examples within the fixed 256-step budget is helping more than additional model changes. The clean next experiment is a further moderate batch-size increase to 96 while keeping the now-strong optimizer and architecture unchanged.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 112","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 96","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last two strongest gains both came from increasing batch size, indicating that this fixed-step setup benefits substantially from processing more examples per update. With only a couple of steps remaining, the highest-value follow-up is another moderate batch-size increase rather than changing the now-strong optimizer or architecture.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 112","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest late-stage trend has been consistent improvement from increasing batch size under the fixed 256-step budget, which effectively exposes the model to more training examples without disturbing the successful optimizer and architecture. With one step left, the highest-probability follow-up is another moderate increase to 128.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}
